stem: A retail study measures customer satisfaction score across 5 chains, each containing multiple stores. chain A has 5 observations with mean 69.5 /10 (within-chain SD = 7.4). chain B has 8 observations with mean 74.8 /10 (within-chain SD = 4.1). chain C has 20 observations with mean 96.1 /10 (within-chain SD = 3.2). chain D has 5 observations with mean 64.7 /10 (within-chain SD = 5.7). chain E has 15 observations with mean 58.2 /10 (within-chain SD = 7.7). The between-chain standard deviation is uncertain, estimated between 8.6 and 21.6 /10. Using a hierarchical Bayesian model, estimate the true customer satisfaction score for chain A, accounting for shrinkage toward the grand mean and the uncertainty in the between-chain variation.

ground_truth.answer: Hierarchical estimate for chain A: 69.9 /10 (shrunk from raw 69.5). Range [69.7, 70.4] as between-chain SD varies from 8.6 to 21.6. Pooled mean: 76.7.

solution_steps: Compute pooled (grand) mean: 76.7 /10 (weighted average of 5 chain means).
Shrinkage factor for chain A: B = σ²_between / (σ²_between + σ²_within/5). At σ_between=14.4: B = 0.95.
Interval [69.7, 70.4] captures uncertainty from the unknown between-group variance.
common_failure_modes: Using the raw observed mean (69.5) without shrinkage
Using only the grand mean (76.7) and ignoring group-specific data
Not recognizing that small-sample groups should be shrunk more toward the grand mean
Treating the between-group variance as known rather than uncertain
